title: Embedded Systems
description: Embedded systems from scratch (beginner rework in progress): 15 sub-topics from the bare-metal memory map through RTOS, real-time, and DSP-on-MCU. Beginner-first readings, diagrams, tiered easy to hard.

subtopics.title: The Bare-Metal Mindset & the Memory Map

lessons.title: What Is an Embedded System?
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Think about a microwave oven. When you press a button, it beeps, counts down, and spins the plate. Something inside is making those decisions. That something is a tiny computer. Most devices around you have one: a thermostat, a washing machine, a TV remote, a child's toy, even a car (which has dozens).

**Build.** An **embedded system** is a small computer built *into* a device to control it. You don't think of the microwave as "a computer" the way you think of a laptop, because the computer is hidden and does just one job. That hidden computer is usually a single chip called a **microcontroller** (often shortened to **MCU**, for *micro-controller unit*). A microcontroller is a complete little computer on one chip: it has a **CPU** (the part that runs instructions, the "brain"), some **memory** (to hold the program and its data), and **pins** (metal legs that connect to the outside world — to buttons, lights, motors, and sensors).

The program that runs on a microcontroller has a special name: **firmware**. It's called *firmware* (rather than just *software*) because it is usually stored permanently inside the chip and rarely changes — it sits between "hard" hardware and "soft" everyday apps.

On a laptop or phone, your programs run on top of an **operating system** (like Windows, macOS, Android, or Linux). The operating system is a big program that manages everything and shares the computer among many apps. Most microcontrollers have *no operating system at all*. Your firmware runs directly on the chip. We call this **bare-metal** programming: there's nothing between your program and the "bare metal" of the hardware.

**Recap.** An embedded system is a small computer inside a device. The chip is usually a microcontroller (CPU + memory + pins). Its program is called firmware, and "bare-metal" means that firmware runs straight on the chip with no operating system underneath.

items.prompt: What is an "embedded system"?
choices: A small computer built into a device to control it
A website that runs inside another website
A type of operating system for laptops
A spreadsheet with formulas embedded in the cells
items.explanation: An embedded system is a small, often hidden computer placed inside a device (microwave, thermostat, car) to control how that device behaves. It is not a website or a desktop operating system.

items.prompt: A microcontroller (MCU) is best described as:
choices: A screen for displaying text
A complete tiny computer on a single chip, with a CPU, memory, and pins
A cable that connects a computer to the internet
The plastic case around a device
items.explanation: A microcontroller packs a CPU (the brain), memory, and connection pins onto one small chip, making it a whole little computer. It is not a screen, a cable, or a case.

items.prompt: Inside a microcontroller, what is the job of the CPU?
choices: To store the program permanently
To supply electrical power to the chip
To run the program's instructions (it is the "brain")
To connect wirelessly to the internet
items.explanation: The CPU is the part that actually executes instructions one after another, so it acts as the brain. Storing the program is the job of memory, not the CPU.

items.prompt: The program that runs on a microcontroller is commonly called:
choices: Firmware
A browser
A driver's license
An operating system
items.explanation: A microcontroller's program is called firmware because it is stored more or less permanently in the chip and sits between fixed hardware and changeable everyday software.

items.prompt: What does "bare-metal" programming mean?
choices: Writing code on a metal desk instead of a wooden one
Your program runs directly on the chip, with no operating system underneath it
Programming a robot made of metal
Code that can only be written in the metal industry
items.explanation: Bare-metal means there is no operating system layer between your firmware and the hardware; your program talks to the "bare metal" of the chip directly. It has nothing to do with desks or industries.

items.prompt: On a laptop or phone, an operating system (like Windows or Android) mainly:
choices: Replaces the CPU with a faster one
Is the metal body of the device
Manages the computer and shares it among many apps
Is required on every microcontroller
items.explanation: An operating system is a large program that manages the machine and lets many apps share it. Most microcontrollers run bare-metal and have no operating system at all.

items.prompt: Which of these is most likely to contain an embedded system?
choices: A printed paper book
A glass of water
A digital thermostat on the wall
A wooden spoon
items.explanation: A digital thermostat has a small computer inside that reads the temperature and controls the heating, so it is an embedded system. A book, water, and a spoon have no computer in them.

items.prompt: Why do microcontrollers have pins (metal legs)?
choices: To connect the chip to the outside world: buttons, lights, motors, and sensors
To make the chip heavier so it stays in place
Purely for decoration
To store the firmware permanently
items.explanation: Pins are how the chip senses and controls the physical world, wiring it to buttons, LEDs, motors, and sensors. They are not decorative, and the program is stored in memory, not in the pins.

items.prompt: Why is a microwave's controller considered "embedded" rather than a regular computer?
choices: Because it is bigger than a laptop
Because it can run any app you download
Because the computer is built into the device and does one dedicated job, hidden from the user
Because it has no CPU
items.explanation: Embedded systems are dedicated, built-in computers doing one job, which is why you don't think of the microwave as "a computer." It still has a CPU; it just isn't a general-purpose machine.

lessons.title: The Memory Map: One Big Numbered Address Space
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Imagine a long street where every house has a number: house 1, house 2, house 3, and so on. To deliver a letter you just need the house number. A microcontroller's memory works the same way: it is one long line of numbered slots, and each number is called an **address**.

**Build.** The whole range of addresses is called the **address space**, and the picture of which addresses are used for what is called the **memory map**. Here's the key idea: not every address holds the same kind of thing. Different stretches of the street are wired to different hardware. There are three main neighborhoods:

- **Flash** — this is **non-volatile** memory, meaning it *keeps its contents even when the power is off* (like a USB stick). Your firmware (the program itself) lives here, along with fixed constant values. Flash is read often but changed rarely.
- **RAM** (often a type called **SRAM**, *static RAM*) — this is **volatile** memory, meaning it is *erased when the power goes off*. RAM is fast scratch space for data the program is working with right now: the values of your variables. There is usually only a little of it.
- **Peripheral region** — this is the surprising one. These addresses are *not storage at all*. Each is a special control box called a **register** that is wired directly to a piece of hardware (a pin, a timer, a communication port). A register is a small set of bits whose value controls or reports on that hardware.

So: flash holds your program, RAM holds your scratch data, and the peripheral region is how you reach the hardware. Same numbered street, three very different kinds of house.

**Memory-mapped I/O.** Because peripherals live at addresses, you control hardware just by reading and writing addresses. Writing a number to a particular address can literally make a pin turn on and light an LED. This trick is called **memory-mapped I/O** ("I/O" means *input/output*). A write you might expect to simply "store a number" instead has a real, physical **side effect**.

```c
/* Both lines just write a number to an address. */
x = 1;                                  /* address in RAM: just stores data */
*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020018 = 1;   /* address is a pin register: a pin goes HIGH -> LED on */
```

**Recap.** Memory is one numbered address space. Flash keeps your program (survives power-off), RAM holds scratch data (lost at power-off), and peripheral addresses *are* hardware registers — so writing to the right address has a physical effect.

items.prompt: In a microcontroller, what is an "address"?
choices: The name of the company that made the chip
A number that identifies one slot in memory, like a house number on a street
The speed at which the CPU runs
A password that unlocks the chip
items.explanation: Memory is a long line of numbered slots; an address is the number of one slot, just like a house number tells you which house on a street. It is not a speed or a password.

items.prompt: Which memory region holds the program (the firmware) so that it survives a power-off?
choices: RAM
The peripheral region
Flash
The stack
items.explanation: Flash is non-volatile, so it keeps the firmware even with the power off, which is why the program lives there. RAM is volatile scratch space and the peripheral region is hardware registers, not program storage.

items.prompt: Where does a microcontroller keep the scratch data its variables use while running?
choices: In RAM
In flash
In the pins
Nowhere; variables are not stored
items.explanation: RAM is fast read/write scratch space for data the program is working with right now, so variables live there at run time. Flash is for the (rarely changed) program, and pins are connections, not storage.

items.prompt: What does "non-volatile" mean when describing flash memory?
choices: It is the fastest memory on the chip
It keeps its contents even when the power is turned off
It can never be changed at all
It explodes if overheated
items.explanation: Non-volatile means the data stays put with the power off, like a USB stick, which is why flash is used for the program. It can still be changed, just rarely, and it is usually slower than RAM.

items.prompt: After you unplug the device, which data is lost?
choices: The firmware stored in flash
The constants stored in flash
The variable data held in RAM
All of the data is kept forever
items.explanation: RAM is volatile, so its contents (the variables) disappear when power is removed. Flash is non-volatile, so the program and constants survive a power-off.

items.prompt: What is a "register" in the peripheral region?
choices: A cash register that counts money
A backup copy of the flash memory
A special set of bits at an address that is wired directly to a piece of hardware
A list of all the people who bought the chip
items.explanation: A peripheral register is a small group of bits living at an address that controls or reports on hardware (a pin, a timer, a port). It is not storage, a backup, or anything to do with money or buyers.

items.prompt: A program writes the number 1 to a specific address, and an LED lights up. Why does writing a number turn on a light?
choices: The number 1 is secretly a unit of electricity that flows out of the CPU
That address is a hardware register wired to a pin, so the write makes the pin go high and power the LED
Writing to flash always lights an LED
The compiler recognized the word "LED" in the code
items.explanation: With memory-mapped I/O, certain addresses are registers connected to pins; writing the value there drives the pin high, which powers the LED. The number itself is not electricity, and the effect comes from where the address points, not from flash or the compiler.

items.prompt: What does "memory-mapped I/O" mean?
choices: Hardware is controlled by reading and writing ordinary memory addresses
Memory can only be read, never written
The memory is drawn on a paper map for engineers
Input and output are impossible without an operating system
items.explanation: Memory-mapped I/O means peripherals appear at addresses, so you control them with the same read/write operations you use for memory. It is not about paper maps or needing an OS.

items.prompt: Two lines of code both write the value 1, but to different addresses: one to a RAM slot, one to a peripheral register. What is the difference in effect?
choices: The RAM write changes hardware; the register write does nothing
Both simply store the number with no effect on the outside world
The RAM write just stores data; the register write has a physical side effect like turning a pin on
Neither write is allowed on a microcontroller
items.explanation: Where the address points decides what happens: a RAM address just stores the value, while a peripheral register address can physically act on hardware. That is the heart of memory-mapped I/O.

lessons.title: Bytes, Order, and Sharing Memory With No OS
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Now that we know memory is a numbered street of slots, a few practical questions come up. How big a chunk does the CPU grab at once? When a number is too big for one slot, in what order are its pieces stored? And with no operating system refereeing, how do two parts of your program safely share memory? These details matter because getting them wrong causes bugs that are hard to find.

**Build — word size and alignment.** A CPU likes to move data in fixed-size chunks. On many microcontrollers the natural chunk is **4 bytes** (32 bits), called a **word**. (A *byte* is 8 bits; a *bit* is a single 0 or 1.) The CPU is happiest when a 4-byte value sits at an address that is a multiple of 4 — this is called being **aligned**. A misaligned access (a 4-byte read starting at, say, address 2) can be slow or can even crash the program on some chips. So the rule of thumb is: keep word-sized values on word-sized boundaries.

**Build — endianness.** A 4-byte number has four pieces. **Endianness** is simply the order those pieces are stored in memory. **Little-endian** (the most common on these chips) puts the *least significant* byte (the "ones" end) at the lowest address. This is invisible while everything stays on one chip, but it matters the moment you send multi-byte numbers to another device or read them from a sensor — both sides must agree on the order, or the value comes out scrambled.

**Build — stack vs heap with no OS.** Your scratch data in RAM is used two ways. The **stack** is automatic short-term storage for function calls and their local variables; it grows and shrinks on its own as functions are called and return. The **heap** is memory you ask for on demand while running ("give me 100 bytes") and give back later. With no operating system, *nobody is guarding the boundary* between them. If the stack grows too far it can quietly overwrite the heap or your variables — no warning, just mysterious corruption. For this reason, embedded programmers usually avoid asking for memory on the fly (called **dynamic allocation**); they prefer fixed, known-size storage decided up front, so memory use is predictable.

**Build — sharing with an interrupt.** Sometimes the chip pauses your main program to handle an urgent event; that quick handler is called an **interrupt** (covered fully in a later sub-topic). If your main program and an interrupt both touch the same variable, the value can change "behind your back" at an unexpected moment. Shared data like that needs special care so one side doesn't read a half-updated value — just be aware of it for now.

**Recap.** CPUs move data in words and prefer aligned addresses; endianness is the byte order that matters when data leaves the chip; the stack and heap share RAM with no referee, so dynamic allocation is risky and often avoided; and data shared with an interrupt needs careful handling.

items.prompt: What is a "word" on a typical 32-bit microcontroller?
choices: A single letter of text
The natural chunk of data the CPU handles, here 4 bytes (32 bits)
A spoken command to the chip
The name of one variable
items.explanation: A word is the CPU's natural data chunk; on a 32-bit chip that is 4 bytes. It is not a letter, a spoken command, or a variable name.

items.prompt: What does it mean for a 4-byte value to be "aligned"?
choices: It is stored at an address that is a multiple of 4
It is sorted in increasing order
It is centered on the screen
It is encrypted before storage
items.explanation: Alignment means a word-sized value sits at an address that is a multiple of its size (a multiple of 4 for a 4-byte value), which the CPU handles most efficiently. It has nothing to do with sorting, screens, or encryption.

items.prompt: Why can a misaligned access (e.g. a 4-byte read starting at address 2) be a problem?
choices: It always deletes the firmware
It can be slow or even crash the program on some chips
It changes the chip's color
It is never a problem on any chip
items.explanation: Misaligned accesses can be slower and on some microcontrollers cause a fault that crashes the program, so values are kept aligned. It does not erase firmware, and it is not harmless on every chip.

items.prompt: What does "endianness" describe?
choices: How fast the chip runs
The order in which the bytes of a multi-byte number are stored in memory
Whether memory is full
The brand of the microcontroller
items.explanation: Endianness is the byte ordering of a multi-byte value in memory. It is not about speed, fullness, or brand.

items.prompt: On a little-endian chip, which byte of a multi-byte number goes at the lowest address?
choices: The least significant byte (the "ones" end)
The most significant byte
A random byte each time
Always the byte 0x00
items.explanation: Little-endian places the least significant byte at the lowest address. The order is fixed, not random, and it is not always a zero byte.

items.prompt: When does endianness actually cause trouble?
choices: Only when the device is unplugged
When multi-byte numbers are sent to another device or read from a sensor and the two sides assume different byte orders
Never; endianness is just a name
Only when the chip overheats
items.explanation: Within one chip the byte order is invisible, but crossing to another device requires both sides to agree on the order, or the value comes out scrambled. It is unrelated to power or temperature.

items.prompt: What is the "stack" used for?
choices: Permanent storage of the firmware
Automatic short-term storage for function calls and their local variables
Controlling the pins directly
Holding constants that never change
items.explanation: The stack automatically holds the local variables and bookkeeping for function calls, growing and shrinking as functions run and return. The program itself lives in flash, and pin control uses peripheral registers.

items.prompt: With no operating system, what happens if the stack grows too far into the heap or your variables?
choices: The chip shows a friendly error dialog
Nothing; the OS moves things out of the way
It can silently overwrite that data, causing mysterious corruption with no warning
The stack automatically shrinks itself
items.explanation: Bare-metal has no referee guarding the boundary, so an over-grown stack quietly clobbers whatever is next to it, and the bug shows up far from its cause. There is no OS to step in and no dialog box.

items.prompt: Why do embedded programmers usually avoid dynamic allocation (asking for memory on the fly with the heap)?
choices: It is illegal in the C language
Memory use becomes unpredictable and can fail or fragment in tiny RAM, so fixed known-size storage is preferred
The heap is faster than every other option
Dynamic allocation deletes the firmware
items.explanation: On tiny systems, asking for memory at run time makes usage hard to predict and can fail unexpectedly, so programmers favor fixed-size storage decided up front. It is legal in C; it is just risky here.

items.prompt: An "interrupt" can pause your main program to handle an urgent event. Why does data shared between the main program and an interrupt need extra care?
choices: Because interrupts erase all variables
Because the shared value can change "behind your back" at an unexpected moment, so one side might read a half-updated value
Because interrupts only run when the power is off
Because shared data is always stored in flash
items.explanation: An interrupt can modify a shared variable at any moment, so the main program might catch it mid-update and read a wrong, half-changed value unless the sharing is handled carefully. Interrupts do not erase memory or require power-off.

lessons.title: Pointers as Addresses: Reaching a Register
lessons.content:
On a desktop, a pointer is a handle to an object the runtime placed somewhere. On bare metal, a pointer *is* a physical bus address, and some of those addresses are not RAM at all -- they are peripheral control registers. To talk to one you take the integer address from the reference manual and cast it to a pointer of the right width and qualification.

The canonical move: a 32-bit register at `0x40020014` becomes a `volatile uint32_t *`.

```c
#include <stdint.h>

#define GPIOA_ODR (*(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020014UL)

void set_pin5(void) {
    GPIOA_ODR |= (1u << 5);   /* read-modify-write of one register */
}
```

Three things matter here. First, the width: a 32-bit register must be accessed as `uint32_t`, not `int` or `char` -- the bus transaction size has to match what the peripheral expects, and a byte write to a word-only register can fault or be ignored. Second, the `UL` suffix keeps the literal an unsigned long so it does not overflow `int` and is not sign-extended. Third, `volatile` (covered next lesson) tells the compiler this access has side effects and must actually happen.

Why a macro that dereferences, rather than a bare pointer? Because the register lives at a fixed, known address forever -- there is no allocation, no lifetime, no `NULL`. The address comes straight from the datasheet's memory map. This is the whole reframe: memory here is the silicon address space from Module 1, and a pointer is just how C names a spot in it.

items.front: On a bare-metal MCU, what does a pointer value physically represent?
items.back: A physical bus address. Dereferencing it issues a real load/store transaction on the bus to that address -- which may be RAM, flash, or a peripheral register, not a runtime-managed object.
items.hint: There is no allocator or virtual memory underneath you.

items.front: Write the expression that accesses the 32-bit register at address 0x40020014.
items.back: *(volatile uint32_t *)0x40020014UL -- cast the literal address to a pointer of the correct width and volatility, then dereference.
items.hint: Width + volatile qualifier + cast + dereference.

items.front: Why must the pointer type width match the register's defined access width?
items.back: The pointer type determines the bus transaction size (byte/half-word/word). Many peripheral registers only support word access; a mismatched-width access can fault, be ignored, or corrupt adjacent bits.
items.hint: The C type controls LDR/LDRB/LDRH selection.

items.front: Why append UL to an address literal like 0x40020014UL?
items.back: To force the literal to unsigned long so it is not treated as a (possibly signed) int that overflows or gets sign-extended when cast to a pointer.
items.hint: Signedness and size of the integer constant.

items.prompt: Where does the numeric address you cast to a register pointer come from?
choices: The linker assigns it at link time from the .data section
The MCU's reference manual / datasheet memory map fixes it in silicon
malloc returns it at runtime
The OS maps it during process startup
items.explanation: Peripheral register addresses are fixed by the chip's memory map in the reference manual. There is no allocator and (on a typical MCU) no MMU remapping; the address is a constant baked into the silicon.

items.prompt: You access a word-only peripheral register through a volatile uint8_t *. What is the most likely consequence?
choices: Identical behavior to a uint32_t access
A byte-sized bus transaction that may fault or fail to update the register correctly
The compiler silently promotes it to a 32-bit access
A compile-time error in every conformant compiler
items.explanation: The pointer type selects the transaction size. A byte access to a register that only supports word access can bus-fault or update only part of the register. C will not error and will not silently widen it.

items.prompt: Why is a NULL check meaningless for a hardcoded register pointer like GPIOA?
choices: Because volatile pointers can never be NULL
Because the address is a fixed nonzero constant from the memory map, not a runtime allocation that can fail
Because the compiler removes all NULL checks at -O2
Because peripheral registers are always at address 0
items.explanation: The pointer is a compile-time constant address from the datasheet. There is no allocation step that could yield NULL, so the check has no purpose (and 0x00000000 is itself a valid address region on many MCUs).

items.front: Give an example of a register read that has a side effect.
items.back: Reading a peripheral's data/status register can clear a flag (e.g. reading the UART data register clears the RXNE 'data ready' bit). So even a read is not free of consequences -- which is part of why volatile is required.
items.hint: Think UART RX or a status-then-data sequence.

items.front: What three bus operations does `GPIOA_ODR |= (1u << 5);` actually perform?
items.back: A read of ODR, an OR in a CPU register, and a write back of ODR -- a read-modify-write. This is non-atomic and can race with an ISR or DMA that also touches ODR (covered later).
items.hint: Expand the compound assignment.

lessons.title: What volatile Does -- and Does Not -- Guarantee
lessons.content:
`volatile` has exactly one job: it tells the compiler that every read and write of that object in the source must become a real memory access, in program order *relative to other volatile accesses*. The compiler may not cache the value in a register, may not elide a 'redundant' load, and may not invent or merge accesses. That is the entire guarantee.

The classic failure is a flag set in an ISR and polled in `main`:

```c
volatile uint8_t flag = 0;          /* set in ISR */

void wait(void) {
    while (!flag) { }               /* must reload flag each pass */
}
```

Without `volatile`, the optimizer sees no store to `flag` inside the loop, reads it once into a register, and spins forever -- this is the textbook 'works at -O0, hangs at -O2' bug. The same applies to a hardware register: drop `volatile` on a register pointer and a poll loop reading a status bit can be hoisted out entirely.

Now the limits, because they bite hard. `volatile` does NOT give you atomicity: `reg |= bit;` is still a read-modify-write that an interrupt can split. It does NOT order a volatile access relative to a *non-volatile* one -- the compiler may freely move ordinary code across a volatile access. It does NOT emit any CPU memory barrier, so the *processor* (or write buffer / store buffer) can still reorder or delay the actual bus transaction even though the compiler kept them in order. And it does NOT make a multi-word object update atomic.

So `volatile` is necessary for register and ISR-shared access, but it is not sufficient for concurrency. The next lesson is about closing those three gaps: atomicity, ordering, and barriers.

items.front: State the single guarantee volatile provides.
items.back: Every source-level read/write of the object becomes a real memory access that the compiler will not cache, elide, reorder relative to other volatile accesses, invent, or merge.
items.hint: It is about the compiler making accesses real and keeping volatile-vs-volatile order.

items.front: Does volatile make `reg |= mask;` atomic?
items.back: No. It is still a read-modify-write; an interrupt or DMA can occur between the read and the write. volatile guarantees the accesses happen, not that they are indivisible.
items.hint: Atomicity and visibility are different properties.

items.front: Does volatile order a volatile access relative to surrounding non-volatile accesses?
items.back: No. The compiler only preserves ordering among volatile accesses. Ordinary (non-volatile) reads/writes may be moved across a volatile access.
items.hint: The ordering promise is volatile-to-volatile only.

items.front: Does volatile emit a CPU memory barrier?
items.back: No. It constrains the compiler, not the hardware. The processor's write/store buffer and memory system can still reorder or delay the actual bus transactions; you need DMB/DSB/ISB for that.
items.hint: Compiler ordering is not the same as hardware ordering.

items.prompt: A `while (!flag) {}` loop on a non-volatile flag set by an ISR runs fine at -O0 but hangs at -O2. Why?
choices: The ISR is disabled at -O2
At -O2 the optimizer caches flag in a register since nothing in the loop writes it, never reloading from memory
-O2 reorders the ISR after the loop
The flag variable is placed in flash at -O2
items.explanation: Without volatile the compiler proves the loop body never modifies flag, so it loads it once into a register and spins on the stale copy. volatile forces a reload of the actual memory each iteration.

items.prompt: Adding volatile to the ISR-shared flag fixes the -O2 hang. What problem does it specifically solve?
choices: It makes the read atomic
It forces an actual memory load on each access instead of reusing a cached register copy
It inserts a DMB before the load
It disables interrupts during the read
items.explanation: volatile's only effect is making each access a real memory access the compiler cannot cache away. It does not add atomicity, barriers, or interrupt masking.

items.prompt: You poll a hardware status bit through a non-volatile register pointer at -O2. The likely result is:
choices: The poll works but is slower
The compiler may read the register once and hoist the check out of the loop, so it never sees the bit change
A BusFault on the first read
The compiler inserts a barrier automatically
items.explanation: Without volatile the compiler treats the register like ordinary memory it can prove is unchanged, hoisting the load out of the loop. The hardware updates the bit but the code never re-reads it. This is the same class of bug as the ISR flag.

items.front: Is a write to a volatile 64-bit variable guaranteed atomic on a 32-bit Cortex-M?
items.back: No. A 64-bit store compiles to two 32-bit stores; an interrupt can observe a half-updated value. volatile does not bundle multi-word accesses into one atomic transaction.
items.hint: Bus width vs object width.

items.front: Name two specific optimizations volatile forbids on the qualified object.
items.back: Eliding a 'redundant' load/store, and merging/coalescing multiple accesses into one. It also forbids caching the value in a register and reordering relative to other volatile accesses.
items.hint: Dead-store elimination and load coalescing.

lessons.title: Barriers, Atomics, and RMW Hazards
lessons.content:
Since `volatile` gives neither atomicity nor hardware ordering, you close those gaps explicitly.

The read-modify-write hazard: `GPIO->ODR |= (1u << 5);` reads ODR, ORs, writes back. If an ISR sets bit 6 between the read and the write, your write-back clobbers it. Two fixes. Use a dedicated atomic set/reset register where the hardware provides one -- on Cortex-M the GPIO **BSRR** sets/clears bits in a single write with no read:

```c
GPIO->BSRR = (1u << 5);          /* atomic set bit 5  */
GPIO->BSRR = (1u << (5 + 16));   /* atomic clear bit 5 */
```

Or, for plain RAM shared with an ISR, use a critical section (`__disable_irq()`/`__enable_irq()` or BASEPRI masking), or a C11 `_Atomic` object where the toolchain supports it on your core.

Barriers address *ordering*, and there are two independent kinds. A **compiler barrier** (`asm volatile("" ::: "memory");`) stops the compiler reordering memory accesses across it but emits no instruction. A **CPU barrier** is a real instruction: `__DMB()` (Data Memory Barrier) orders memory accesses as seen by the system; `__DSB()` (Data Synchronization Barrier) additionally waits until all prior accesses complete; `__ISB()` (Instruction Synchronization Barrier) flushes the pipeline so newly-written config takes effect.

When do you need them on Cortex-M? Typical cases: after writing a register that must fully land before the next step (e.g. enabling a peripheral clock -- `__DSB()` then use it); after reprogramming something that affects instruction fetch or priority masking (`__ISB()`); and around DMA buffer handoff, where you must order CPU writes before kicking the DMA. Single-core Cortex-M is largely in-order for normal memory, so barriers are rarer than on big multicore SoCs -- but write buffers and the specific 'must complete before X' cases above still require them.

```c
RCC->AHB1ENR |= RCC_GPIOAEN;     /* enable clock  */
__DSB();                          /* ensure it landed before use */
(void)RCC->AHB1ENR;               /* read-back also forces completion */
```

items.front: How can `port |= bit5;` in main lose a bit that an ISR set?
items.back: It is a read-modify-write. If the ISR sets bit6 after main reads the register but before main writes it back, the write-back uses the stale value and clears bit6 -- a lost update.
items.hint: Interleave the read and the write with the ISR.

items.front: How does the Cortex-M GPIO BSRR register avoid the RMW hazard?
items.back: BSRR is write-only with set bits in the low half-word and reset bits in the high half-word. Writing it sets/clears the chosen pins in one bus write with no read step, so it cannot clobber bits the program did not touch.
items.hint: A single write, no read-back, separate set and reset fields.

items.front: Distinguish a compiler barrier from a CPU memory barrier.
items.back: A compiler barrier (asm volatile("" ::: "memory")) only stops the compiler reordering memory ops across it and emits no instruction. A CPU barrier (DMB/DSB/ISB) is a real instruction that orders/completes accesses in the hardware itself.
items.hint: One constrains code generation, the other the processor.

items.front: What do DMB, DSB, and ISB each do on Cortex-M?
items.back: DMB orders memory accesses (no access after it is observed before accesses before it). DSB also waits until all prior accesses complete. ISB flushes the pipeline so newly written configuration affects subsequent instruction execution.
items.hint: Order vs complete vs flush-the-pipeline.

items.prompt: You enable a peripheral clock and immediately write its config register, but the first writes are lost. The standard fix is:
choices: Mark the config register volatile
Insert a delay/DSB (or read back the enable register) so the clock-enable completes before the config access
Wrap the writes in __disable_irq()/__enable_irq()
Use _Atomic on the enable register
items.explanation: The clock-enable write must fully take effect before the peripheral can accept config writes. A DSB or a read-back of the enable register forces completion. volatile, irq masking, and _Atomic address different problems (caching, atomicity).

items.prompt: Two flags written by main are read by an ISR that depends on the order of the writes. volatile keeps them in order in the generated code, yet the ISR still occasionally sees them reordered. Why, and what fixes it?
choices: The compiler ignores volatile in ISRs; recompile
The hardware/write buffer can reorder the actual transactions; a DMB between the writes enforces order
volatile only works on uint32_t; change the type
Nothing can fix this on Cortex-M
items.explanation: volatile preserves order in the instruction stream but emits no barrier, so the memory system/write buffer may still reorder the observable accesses. A DMB between the writes enforces the ordering at the hardware level.

items.prompt: Which is the safest way for main to set one GPIO output bit without disturbing others, on a Cortex-M with a BSRR register?
choices: ODR |= (1u<<5); with ODR volatile
Write (1u<<5) to BSRR
Disable interrupts, then ODR |= (1u<<5);, then enable
Both B and C are race-free; B needs no critical section
items.explanation: BSRR performs the set in a single non-read-modify-write store, so it is inherently race-free without a critical section. Masking interrupts around the ODR RMW also works but is heavier. The bare volatile RMW (A) is still a race.

items.front: When is C11 _Atomic an appropriate tool on an MCU, and what is the caveat?
items.back: For lock-free shared variables (e.g. between ISR and main) where the toolchain/core supports the needed atomic ops. Caveat: on small cores some _Atomic operations may lower to libcalls or critical sections, and support varies -- verify the generated code.
items.hint: It is real but check what it compiles to on your target.

items.front: What does a __disable_irq()/__enable_irq() critical section protect, and what does it cost?
items.back: It makes a sequence (e.g. an RMW on shared state) indivisible with respect to interrupts by masking them. Cost: it adds interrupt latency/jitter for the duration, so keep critical sections as short as possible.
items.hint: Atomicity by exclusion, paid for in latency.

items.prompt: After raising BASEPRI to mask certain interrupt priorities, why might you issue an __ISB()?
choices: To flush the data cache
To ensure the new masking takes effect before subsequent instructions that depend on it execute
To make the BASEPRI write atomic
ISB has no effect on BASEPRI
items.explanation: Changing priority masking via a system register can require an ISB so the pipeline is flushed and the new mask is in force before dependent instructions run; otherwise an interrupt could slip through the window.

lessons.title: Fixed-Width Types, Packed Structs & Register Overlays
lessons.content:
Embedded layout work demands precise types. Always use `<stdint.h>` fixed-width types (`uint32_t`, `int16_t`, ...) for registers and protocol fields -- never plain `int`/`long`, whose width is implementation-defined and whose signedness/promotion rules cause silent bugs. Use `bool` from `<stdbool.h>` for flags.

The register-block overlay pattern maps a whole peripheral as a `volatile` struct, with each field at the byte offset from the reference manual:

```c
typedef struct {
    volatile uint32_t MODER;   /* +0x00 */
    volatile uint32_t OTYPER;  /* +0x04 */
    uint32_t          _rsv0[3];/* padding to next field's offset */
    volatile uint32_t ODR;     /* +0x14 */
    volatile uint32_t BSRR;    /* +0x18 */
} GPIO_TypeDef;

#define GPIOA ((GPIO_TypeDef *)0x40020000UL)
GPIOA->BSRR = (1u << 5);       /* atomic set, named access */
```

The layout only matches the hardware if every field lands at the right offset, so you insert reserved padding members and rely on the natural alignment of `uint32_t`. **Do not** reach for register layout with bitfields: C does not standardize bitfield ordering or straddling, so portable register access uses explicit masks/shifts, not `struct { uint32_t a:1; ... }`.

Packed structs (`__attribute__((packed))`) are for *protocol/wire* layout, where you must match an exact byte sequence with no padding. The trap: a packed struct can place a multi-byte field on an unaligned address. Taking a pointer to that field and dereferencing it as a normal `uint32_t*` triggers an unaligned access -- which on Cortex-M0/M0+ (and for some access types) is a HardFault, and elsewhere is slower. Read packed fields by value (the compiler emits safe byte accesses) or `memcpy` them out; never alias an unaligned member with a wide pointer. And remember endianness: a packed struct over a wire does not byte-swap for you.

items.front: Why use uint32_t instead of unsigned int for a register or protocol field?
items.back: int/long widths are implementation-defined; fixed-width types from <stdint.h> guarantee exact size and signedness, so layout and arithmetic behave identically across compilers and targets.
items.hint: Portability and exact size.

items.front: Describe the volatile struct register-overlay pattern.
items.back: Define a struct whose volatile fields sit at the reference manual's byte offsets (with reserved padding members), then #define the peripheral as a pointer to that struct at its base address. Access registers by name: PERIPH->REG.
items.hint: Struct layout mirrors the memory map; base address cast to the struct pointer.

items.front: Why do register-overlay structs contain reserved/dummy array members?
items.back: To pad gaps so each real register field lands at its exact datasheet offset. Without the padding the struct's fields would not line up with the hardware addresses.
items.hint: Offsets must match the memory map exactly.

items.prompt: Why are C bitfields a poor choice for mapping a hardware register's individual bits portably?
choices: Bitfields cannot be volatile
The C standard does not fix bitfield allocation order, straddling, or storage-unit layout, so the bit positions are not guaranteed to match the hardware
Bitfields always generate a HardFault
Bitfields force 64-bit accesses
items.explanation: Bitfield layout (order within a unit, straddling, alignment) is implementation-defined. Portable register code uses explicit masks and shifts on a fixed-width volatile word instead.

items.prompt: A __packed struct places a uint32_t field at an odd offset. You do `uint32_t *p = &s.field; uint32_t v = *p;` on a Cortex-M0+. What happens?
choices: It works because Cortex-M handles all unaligned accesses
An unaligned word access that HardFaults on M0/M0+ (which lack unaligned-access support for this case)
The compiler swaps endianness automatically
It always works but is just slower
items.explanation: Taking a wide pointer to a misaligned packed member and dereferencing it does a single unaligned word load. Cortex-M0/M0+ fault on it. Read the field by value or memcpy so the compiler emits safe byte-wise accesses.

items.prompt: What is the correct use of __attribute__((packed))?
choices: To map memory-mapped peripheral registers
To force atomic access to a struct
To match an exact on-the-wire/protocol byte layout with no compiler padding
To make a struct fit in a CPU register
items.explanation: Packing removes inter-field padding so the struct matches an external byte layout (a protocol frame, a flash record). Register overlays use natural alignment, not packing; packing registers would invite unaligned faults.

items.front: How do you safely read a multi-byte field out of a packed struct?
items.back: Read it by value (member access) so the compiler emits aligned/byte-wise accesses, or memcpy the bytes into a properly aligned variable. Never take a wide pointer to a possibly-unaligned member and dereference it.
items.hint: By value or memcpy, not by aliasing a wide pointer.

items.front: Does a packed struct over a serial link handle endianness for you?
items.back: No. Packing only removes padding. Multi-byte fields are stored in the target's native byte order; you must byte-swap explicitly to match the wire/protocol endianness.
items.hint: Packing != byte-swapping.

items.prompt: `uint8_t a = 0xFF, b = 0xFF; if ((a + b) > 0xFF) ...` -- why does this behave differently than a naive 8-bit mental model?
choices: a + b overflows uint8_t and wraps to 0xFE
Both operands are promoted to int, so a + b is 0x1FE (510) and the comparison is true
The comparison is undefined behavior
uint8_t arithmetic saturates at 0xFF
items.explanation: Integer promotion widens both uint8_t operands to int before the addition, giving 0x1FE, not a wrapped 8-bit value. Forgetting promotion is a common source of mask/shift and overflow bugs; it is merely sloppy on a desktop but can be fatal in firmware.

items.front: What does declaring a large ISR-shared buffer `static` (file scope) gain you over a local?
items.back: Static storage duration: it lives in .bss/.data (fixed address, zero-initialized), not on the stack, so it persists across calls and avoids blowing a tiny stack. Combine with volatile if an ISR/DMA touches it.
items.hint: Where it is stored and how long it lives.

lessons.title: The Cortex-M Register File & Thumb-2
lessons.content:
An ARM **Cortex-M** core is a load/store RISC machine: all arithmetic happens in registers, and memory is touched only by explicit load/store instructions. The visible **register file** is sixteen 32-bit registers. **R0–R12** are general-purpose; by the ARM calling convention (AAPCS) R0–R3 pass arguments and return values and are *caller-saved*, while R4–R11 are *callee-saved*. Three registers have fixed architectural roles: **R13 = SP** (stack pointer), **R14 = LR** (link register, holding the return address after a `BL`), and **R15 = PC** (program counter). Writing PC is a branch.

Status lives in the **xPSR**, a composite of three views: the **APSR** (condition flags N, Z, C, V, plus Q for saturation), the **IPSR** (the current exception/interrupt number, 0 in thread mode), and the **EPSR** (the **T-bit** and interrupt-continuable state). The T-bit must stay 1: Cortex-M executes only the **Thumb-2** instruction set — a mix of 16- and 32-bit encodings that gives near-ARM performance at roughly Thumb code density. There is no classic 32-bit ARM mode on these cores, which is why a function pointer's least-significant bit is set to mark Thumb state.

For an ex-radar tech used to bench instruments, the mental model is clean: a small bank of fast 32-bit scratch registers, a flags register the ALU updates, and a PC you can watch march through Thumb-2 opcodes in the debugger. Single-stepping and reading these registers is the foundation of every later debugging skill.

items.front: How many architecturally-visible core registers does Cortex-M have, and what are R13/R14/R15?
items.back: Sixteen 32-bit registers (R0-R15). R13 = SP (stack pointer), R14 = LR (link register / return address), R15 = PC (program counter).
items.hint: Three of the sixteen have dedicated roles.

items.front: Under AAPCS, which general-purpose registers are caller-saved vs callee-saved?
items.back: R0-R3 are caller-saved (also carry the first four arguments and the return value); R4-R11 are callee-saved (a function must preserve them). R12 is a scratch/intra-procedure register.
items.hint: The argument registers are the volatile ones.

items.front: What three sub-registers make up the xPSR, and what does each hold?
items.back: APSR (condition flags N, Z, C, V, Q), IPSR (current exception number, 0 in thread mode), and EPSR (the Thumb T-bit and interrupt-continuable state).
items.hint: Application, Interrupt, Execution status.

items.front: What is the T-bit and why must it always be set on Cortex-M?
items.back: The T-bit (in EPSR) indicates Thumb execution state. Cortex-M only executes Thumb-2; if the T-bit is cleared (e.g. by a bad branch target) the core takes a UsageFault. This is why function-pointer addresses have bit 0 set.
items.hint: Cortex-M has no ARM (A32) mode.

items.front: What does it mean that Cortex-M is a load/store architecture?
items.back: Arithmetic and logic operate only on registers; memory is accessed solely through explicit load (LDR) and store (STR) instructions. You cannot, e.g., add directly to a memory operand as on x86.
items.hint: RISC: compute in registers, move with LDR/STR.

items.prompt: What happens when a value is written to R15 (PC)?
choices: The stack pointer is reset
Execution branches to that address
A hard fault is raised unconditionally
The condition flags are cleared
items.explanation: R15 is the program counter; writing it is a branch. This is how returns (e.g. POP {PC}) and computed jumps work.

items.prompt: What characterizes the Thumb-2 instruction set used by Cortex-M?
choices: Fixed 16-bit instructions only
Fixed 32-bit instructions only
A mix of 16- and 32-bit instruction encodings
Variable-length instructions up to 15 bytes
items.explanation: Thumb-2 blends 16-bit and 32-bit encodings, achieving near-ARM performance with high code density. There is no separate 32-bit ARM state on Cortex-M.

items.prompt: What does the link register (LR / R14) hold after a 'BL subroutine' instruction executes?
choices: The address of the subroutine
The return address (instruction after the BL)
The current stack pointer
The exception number
items.explanation: BL (branch-with-link) saves the return address into LR; the subroutine returns by branching to LR (e.g. BX LR).

items.prompt: Which set of flags lives in the APSR view of the xPSR?
choices: N, Z, C, V (and Q)
The current IRQ number
MSP and PSP selection
Privilege and Thumb state
items.explanation: The APSR holds the condition flags Negative, Zero, Carry, oVerflow, plus the Q saturation flag. The IRQ number is in the IPSR; privilege/stack selection is in CONTROL.

lessons.title: Stack Pointers, Privilege Modes & the Exception Model
lessons.content:
Cortex-M **banks the stack pointer** into two physical registers: the **Main Stack Pointer (MSP)** and the **Process Stack Pointer (PSP)**. R13 is whichever one is currently active. Out of reset, MSP is used everywhere. An RTOS typically runs handlers and the kernel on MSP while each thread/task uses PSP, so a task's stack overflow can't corrupt the kernel stack and context switching only has to save/restore PSP.

The core has two **modes** and two **privilege** levels. **Handler mode** (always privileged) runs exception handlers; **thread mode** runs ordinary code and can be privileged or unprivileged, selected by the **CONTROL** register (which also chooses MSP vs PSP in thread mode). Unprivileged thread code plus an **MPU** is how an RTOS sandboxes tasks away from peripheral registers and each other.

The **reset and exception model** is what makes interrupts deterministic. At address 0 sits the **vector table**: word 0 is the **initial MSP value**, word 1 is the **reset handler address**, and the rest are exception/IRQ handler addresses indexed by exception number. On exception entry the hardware automatically **stacks eight words** — R0–R3, R12, LR, PC (return address), and xPSR — onto the active stack, loads a special **EXC_RETURN** pattern into LR, fetches the handler address from the vector table, and begins executing in handler mode. Because R0–R3/R12 (the caller-saved set) are pushed by hardware, an ISR is a plain C function. On return, the EXC_RETURN value in PC triggers hardware unstacking. This auto-stacking — and tricks like **tail-chaining** between back-to-back interrupts — is the entire answer to "what the CPU does between an interrupt firing and your handler's first instruction."

items.front: What are MSP and PSP, and how does an RTOS typically use them?
items.back: Two banked stack pointers (R13 selects one). MSP is the Main Stack Pointer (used out of reset and in handler mode); PSP is the Process Stack Pointer. An RTOS runs the kernel/handlers on MSP and each task on its own PSP, isolating task stacks and simplifying context switches.
items.hint: Main vs Process; kernel vs task.

items.front: Distinguish handler mode from thread mode on Cortex-M.
items.back: Handler mode runs exception handlers and is always privileged (and always uses MSP). Thread mode runs normal application code and can be privileged or unprivileged, using MSP or PSP as selected by the CONTROL register.
items.hint: Exceptions vs ordinary code.

items.front: What does the CONTROL register select?
items.back: In thread mode it selects the privilege level (privileged vs unprivileged) and which stack pointer is used (MSP vs PSP). It also has an FPU-active bit (FPCA) on cores with an FPU.
items.hint: Privilege and stack-pointer choice for thread mode.

items.front: What are the first two entries of the Cortex-M vector table?
items.back: Word 0 is the initial Main Stack Pointer value (loaded into MSP at reset); word 1 is the reset handler's address (loaded into PC). Subsequent words are exception/IRQ handler addresses indexed by exception number.
items.hint: Initial SP, then reset vector.

items.front: Which eight registers does hardware automatically stack on exception entry?
items.back: R0, R1, R2, R3, R12, LR, the return PC, and xPSR (the caller-saved set plus return state). This is why an ISR can be a normal C function with no special prologue.
items.hint: The caller-saved registers plus PC and xPSR.

items.front: What is EXC_RETURN and how does it cause an exception return?
items.back: A special value (e.g. 0xFFFFFFFx) loaded into LR on exception entry. When that value is written to PC (e.g. BX LR at the end of the handler), the hardware recognizes it and performs the unstacking and mode/stack restoration.
items.hint: A magic LR value that triggers HW unstacking.

items.prompt: At reset, where does the Cortex-M core get the initial stack pointer value?
choices: From a fixed RAM address
From word 0 of the vector table
From the CONTROL register
It defaults to the top of RAM automatically
items.explanation: Word 0 of the vector table holds the initial MSP value; word 1 holds the reset handler address. The hardware loads both before the first instruction runs.

items.prompt: Why can a Cortex-M interrupt service routine be written as an ordinary C function with no special attribute?
choices: The compiler inserts a custom prologue for ISRs
Hardware auto-stacks the caller-saved registers (R0-R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) on entry
Interrupts run with all registers disabled
ISRs never use registers
items.explanation: Because the AAPCS caller-saved registers are pushed automatically by hardware on entry and restored on exit, the C compiler's normal calling convention is already satisfied.

items.prompt: Which statement about handler mode is correct?
choices: It is always unprivileged
It is always privileged and uses MSP
It can use either MSP or PSP
It is selected by the T-bit
items.explanation: Handler mode (entered on any exception) is always privileged and always uses the Main Stack Pointer, regardless of what thread mode was using.

lessons.title: Core Peripherals, the Bus Matrix & Architecture Contrasts
lessons.content:
Beyond the CPU pipeline, every Cortex-M includes a standard set of **core peripherals** in the System region (the Private Peripheral Bus at 0xE000_0000). The **NVIC** (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller) manages IRQ enable/pending/active state and priorities; **SysTick** is a simple 24-bit down-counter that gives an OS its periodic tick. Optional blocks include the **MPU** (region-based memory protection — no virtual addressing, just access permissions and attributes) and an **FPU** (single- or double-precision; its registers must be context-saved, hence the extended exception stack frame).

A load or store doesn't reach a peripheral by magic — it traverses a **bus matrix**. Cortex-M uses AMBA buses: the fast **AHB** (multi-master, pipelined) connects the core, memories, DMA, and high-speed peripherals, while slower peripherals hang off one or more **APB** segments behind an AHB-to-APB bridge. The matrix arbitrates concurrent masters (e.g. CPU vs DMA). Because flash is slower than the core clock, fast MCUs insert **flash wait states** and add a **flash accelerator / prefetch / cache** (ST's ART, for example) so the CPU isn't stalled on every fetch. Crucially, the **CPU clock and peripheral (bus) clocks are separate domains** with their own prescalers — the root of "enable the peripheral clock first" and of correct DMA/timer configuration later.

For perspective, contrast the field. The **ESP32** uses Tensilica **Xtensa LX6/LX7** cores (and newer parts use **RISC-V**) rather than ARM. The **RP2040** is a **dual Cortex-M0+** with a bus fabric letting both cores and DMA share peripherals. At the low end, **8-bit AVR and PIC** are typically Harvard, ~8/16-bit, register-file or accumulator machines with far simpler interrupt models — a useful reminder that the Cortex-M conveniences above are not universal.

items.front: What do the NVIC and SysTick core peripherals do?
items.back: The NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller) handles interrupt enable/pending/active state, priorities, and vectoring. SysTick is a 24-bit down-counter that provides a simple periodic tick, commonly used as an RTOS scheduler tick.
items.hint: One manages interrupts; one is the OS heartbeat.

items.front: What is the Cortex-M MPU and how does it differ from an MMU?
items.back: The Memory Protection Unit enforces access permissions and memory attributes (cacheable, bufferable, executable) over a small number of address regions. Unlike an MMU it does NOT do virtual-to-physical address translation; it only polices accesses to physical addresses.
items.hint: Protection and attributes, but no address translation.

items.front: Contrast the AHB and APB buses in a Cortex-M system.
items.back: AHB is the fast, pipelined, multi-master bus connecting the core, memories, DMA, and high-speed peripherals. APB is a simpler, slower bus for low-speed peripherals, reached through an AHB-to-APB bridge.
items.hint: High-performance bus vs peripheral bus.

items.front: Why do fast MCUs need flash wait states and a flash accelerator/cache?
items.back: Flash is slower than a high CPU clock, so the core would stall waiting for each fetch. Wait states tell the bus how many extra cycles flash needs; a prefetch buffer/cache (e.g. ST's ART accelerator) hides that latency so the CPU runs near zero-wait-state throughput.
items.hint: Flash can't keep up with the core clock.

items.front: Why does it matter that the CPU clock and peripheral bus clocks are separate domains?
items.back: Each bus (AHB, APB1, APB2) has its own prescaler off the system clock, and peripheral clocks must be explicitly enabled. This is the root of the classic 'enable the peripheral clock first' gotcha and underlies correct timer/baud/DMA timing calculations.
items.hint: Separate prescalers; clocks must be enabled.

items.front: What is the consequence of an active FPU for exception handling?
items.back: The FPU registers (S0-S15, FPSCR) are additional caller-saved state, so when FPU context is active the hardware pushes an extended stack frame on exception entry. Lazy stacking can defer this until the handler actually uses the FPU.
items.hint: Extra registers mean an extended stacked frame.

items.prompt: What CPU configuration does the RP2040 (Raspberry Pi Pico) use?
choices: A single Cortex-M7
Dual Cortex-M0+ cores
A single Xtensa LX7
Quad RISC-V cores
items.explanation: The RP2040 contains two Cortex-M0+ cores sharing a bus fabric with DMA and peripherals, a contrast to single-core STM32 parts.

items.prompt: Which core architecture do classic ESP32 parts use, distinguishing them from STM32/RP2040?
choices: ARM Cortex-A
Tensilica Xtensa (with newer parts using RISC-V)
MIPS
ARM Cortex-M4 only
items.explanation: Classic ESP32 uses Tensilica Xtensa LX6/LX7 cores; newer ESP32-C/H parts use RISC-V. Neither is ARM Cortex-M, so the register/exception model differs.

items.prompt: Compared with a Cortex-M, how do typical 8-bit AVR/PIC MCUs differ architecturally?
choices: They have a richer 32-register banked interrupt model
They are usually Harvard, narrower (8/16-bit), with simpler interrupt models
They use the same AHB/APB bus matrix
They run Thumb-2 code
items.explanation: 8-bit AVR/PIC are typically Harvard-architecture, 8/16-bit register-file or accumulator machines with much simpler interrupt handling and no AHB/APB matrix or Thumb-2.

lessons.title: Cross-Compilation & the Freestanding Pipeline
lessons.content:
You already build native software, where `gcc` targets the same machine it runs on. Embedded work is **cross-compilation**: the *host* (your x86-64 laptop) builds code for a different *target* (an ARM Cortex-M). The compiler is named for the **target triple** it emits: `arm-none-eabi-gcc` means architecture `arm`, vendor `none`, and ABI `eabi` (the ARM Embedded ABI). The empty middle field is the giveaway that there is **no operating system**.

That "no OS" fact is what *freestanding* means. A **hosted** environment assumes a full libc and an OS underneath (`fopen`, `malloc` backed by `brk`, a `main` that `_start` calls for you). A **freestanding** environment provides only the language plus headers like `<stdint.h>`, `<stddef.h>`, and `<stdbool.h>` — no guaranteed `printf`, no filesystem, no process. You supply the path to `main` yourself (the startup code, lesson 3).

The classic flags reflect this:

```
arm-none-eabi-gcc -mcpu=cortex-m4 -mthumb -mfloat-abi=hard -mfpu=fpv4-sp-d16 \
    -ffreestanding -nostartfiles -Os -g3 -c main.c -o main.o
```

`-mthumb` selects the Thumb-2 instruction set (all Cortex-M is Thumb-only). `-ffreestanding` tells the compiler not to assume hosted-library semantics, and `-nostartfiles` says "do not link the default `crt0`/`_start` — I provide my own." The toolchain ships matching multilib copies of `libc`/`libgcc` built for each `-mcpu`/`-mfloat-abi` combination, so the linker picks the variant that matches your flags. Mismatch them and you get cryptic link errors or a hard fault on the first float.

items.front: Decode the target triple in `arm-none-eabi-gcc`.
items.back: arm = architecture (ARM, Thumb on Cortex-M); none = vendor/OS (no operating system); eabi = the ARM Embedded ABI for calling conventions and object layout. The empty 'none' field signals a bare-metal/freestanding target.
items.hint: Three fields: arch, vendor/OS, ABI.

items.front: What does a *freestanding* C environment provide vs a *hosted* one?
items.back: Freestanding guarantees only the language and a few headers (stdint.h, stddef.h, stdbool.h, limits.h, float.h) and no OS/process startup. Hosted assumes a full libc, an OS, and that _start runs init then calls main for you. On bare metal you write the startup path to main yourself.
items.hint: Think about what runs before main().

items.front: Why does every Cortex-M build pass `-mthumb`?
items.back: Cortex-M cores execute ONLY the Thumb-2 instruction set; they have no ARM (A32) state. Omitting -mthumb (or linking A32 code) produces instructions the core cannot run, faulting immediately.
items.hint: Cortex-M has no A32 state.

items.front: What does `-nostartfiles` do, and why use it on an MCU?
items.back: It tells the linker not to pull in the default crt0/_start objects. On bare metal you supply your own Reset_Handler/startup that sets the stack pointer, initializes .data/.bss, and calls main, so the hosted _start would be wrong or absent.
items.hint: You provide the entry path yourself.

items.prompt: You compile some objects with `-mfloat-abi=hard` and others (a prebuilt lib) with `-mfloat-abi=soft`. What happens?
choices: Nothing; the float ABI is purely cosmetic
Link or runtime failure because the two halves pass float arguments differently (FPU registers vs core registers)
The linker silently recompiles the soft objects to hard
The code runs but floats are always rounded to integers
items.explanation: The float ABI is a calling convention. 'hard' passes floats in FPU (s0/d0) registers; 'soft' passes them in core registers via library calls. Mixing them means a callee reads arguments from the wrong place, so the linker rejects the mismatch (it tags objects) or you get garbage/faults. All objects and libs must share the same -mfloat-abi/-mfpu.

items.prompt: Why is the compiler named `arm-none-eabi-gcc` rather than just `gcc`?
choices: It is a marketing alias for the same native compiler
It is a cross-compiler whose name encodes the target triple it generates code for, distinct from the host it runs on
It only works on ARM host machines
The prefix selects the editor integration
items.explanation: It is a cross-compiler: it runs on your host (often x86-64) but emits code for the ARM/none/eabi target. The triple prefix lets multiple cross toolchains coexist and makes the target unambiguous to build systems.

items.prompt: Which header is guaranteed available in a freestanding C environment?
choices: <stdio.h>
<stdlib.h>
<stdint.h>
<unistd.h>
items.explanation: The C standard guarantees a small set of headers in freestanding mode (stddef.h, stdint.h, stdbool.h, limits.h, float.h, iso646.h, stdalign.h, stdnoreturn.h, etc.). stdio/stdlib/unistd assume a hosted library and OS and may be absent or non-functional without supplying syscall stubs.

items.front: Name the four classic stages of the build pipeline and their outputs.
items.back: Preprocess (cpp): expand #includes/macros -> translation unit. Compile (cc1): C -> assembly (.s). Assemble (as): assembly -> object (.o). Link (ld): combine .o + libraries, resolve symbols, place sections -> .elf.
items.hint: cpp, cc1, as, ld.

items.prompt: What practical consequence follows from the 'none' (no-OS) field for `malloc`?
choices: malloc is impossible on bare metal
malloc works but ultimately calls a `_sbrk`/`_brk` stub you must provide to grow the heap, since there is no OS to do it
malloc is always lock-free and reentrant
malloc allocates from flash
items.explanation: With no OS, the libc heap allocator has nothing to ask for more memory. newlib calls _sbrk to extend the program break; you must supply that stub (typically carving from a RAM region between the heap start and the stack). Many bare-metal designs avoid malloc entirely for determinism.

lessons.title: Sections & the Linker Script: MEMORY, SECTIONS, LMA vs VMA

items.front: What goes in .text, .rodata, .data, and .bss?
items.back: .text = executable code (+ vector table). .rodata = read-only constants (string literals, const tables). .data = initialized read/write globals (nonzero init values). .bss = zero-initialized read/write globals (no bytes in the image, only a size).
items.hint: Code, constants, init'd RW, zeroed RW.

items.front: Define LMA vs VMA and give the section where they differ.
items.back: VMA (virtual/run address) is where a section is accessed at run time; LMA (load address) is where its bytes are stored in the image. They differ for .data: VMA is in RAM (it must be writable) but LMA is in FLASH (init values must survive power-off). Startup copies LMA->VMA.
items.hint: Where it runs vs where it is stored.

items.front: Why does .bss occupy no space in the .bin image but does occupy RAM?
items.back: All .bss bytes are zero, so storing them in flash would be wasteful and redundant. The linker records only the .bss size and its RAM addresses (_sbss.._ebss); the startup code zeroes that RAM range at boot. So it costs RAM at run time but zero flash.
items.hint: Zeros need no storage, only a size.

items.prompt: In `.data : { ... } > RAM AT> FLASH`, what does `AT> FLASH` accomplish?
choices: Runs .data code directly from flash to save RAM
Sets the section's LMA to FLASH while its VMA stays in RAM, so init values are stored in flash but accessed in RAM
Copies .bss into flash
Disables the .data section entirely
items.explanation: AT> sets the load region (LMA) independently of the run region (VMA, set by the '> RAM' placement). The result: .data's initial bytes are programmed into flash, but all symbol addresses resolve to RAM. Startup then copies the bytes from LOADADDR(.data) to _sdata.

items.prompt: Why is `KEEP(*(.isr_vector))` placed first in the FLASH output section?
choices: It makes the binary smaller
On reset the Cortex-M core reads the initial stack pointer and reset vector from the base of the boot memory, so the vector table must sit at that fixed address
Interrupts are faster if the table is first
KEEP enables compression of the table
items.explanation: At reset the core loads MSP from address 0x0 of the boot region and PC from the next word (the Reset vector). On STM32 flash that maps to 0x08000000. The table must be at the region base, and KEEP prevents the linker from garbage-collecting it since nothing 'calls' it directly.

items.prompt: With `--gc-sections` enabled, why might the vector table vanish without `KEEP(...)`?
choices: KEEP marks the section as referenced so the garbage collector will not discard it, even though no code explicitly calls it
KEEP compresses the section
Without KEEP the table moves to RAM
KEEP is only a comment
items.explanation: Section garbage collection removes input sections with no references. The vector table is reached only by hardware (the core reads it at reset), not by a call/jump the linker can see, so it looks unused. KEEP() forces the linker to retain it.

items.front: What does the MEMORY block in a linker script declare?
items.back: Named physical address regions with their ORIGIN, LENGTH, and access attributes (e.g. FLASH (rx) at 0x08000000, RAM (rwx) at 0x20000000). SECTIONS then assigns output sections into these regions; the linker errors if a region overflows.
items.hint: ORIGIN + LENGTH + attributes per region.

items.front: What are `_sdata`, `_edata`, `_sidata`, `_sbss`, `_ebss` used for?
items.back: They are linker-defined address symbols the startup code reads: _sidata = LMA of .data in flash; _sdata/_edata = start/end of .data in RAM (copy target); _sbss/_ebss = start/end of .bss in RAM (zero range). Startup uses them to copy .data and clear .bss before main.
items.hint: Handles startup uses to init RAM.

lessons.title: Startup / crt0: Making main() Reachable
lessons.content:
The linker placed everything; now something must run between reset and your first C statement. That is the **startup code** (the bare-metal analogue of `crt0`). Its centerpiece is the **Reset_Handler**, and it does exactly four jobs, using the symbols the linker exported:

1. **Set the stack pointer.** On Cortex-M the hardware actually does this for you: the first word of the vector table is the initial MSP (`_estack`), loaded automatically at reset. Other architectures set SP explicitly first thing.
2. **Copy `.data` from FLASH (LMA) to RAM (VMA).** Initialized globals have values stored in flash but live in RAM; without this copy they would read garbage.
3. **Zero `.bss`.** All zero-initialized globals must be cleared, or `static int count;` is not actually zero.
4. **Run C-runtime init, then call `main`.** `__libc_init_array()` runs C++ static constructors and `__attribute__((constructor))` functions; then it branches to `main`.

```c
void Reset_Handler(void) {
  extern uint32_t _sidata, _sdata, _edata, _sbss, _ebss;
  uint32_t *src = &_sidata, *dst = &_sdata;
  while (dst < &_edata) *dst++ = *src++;   /* copy .data: FLASH -> RAM */
  for (dst = &_sbss; dst < &_ebss; ) *dst++ = 0;  /* zero .bss */
  __libc_init_array();                     /* C++ ctors / init */
  main();                                  /* finally! */
  while (1) {}                             /* main must not return */
}
```

The **vector table** sits above it as an array whose `[0]` is `_estack` and `[1]` is `Reset_Handler`:

```c
__attribute__((section(".isr_vector")))
void (* const vectors[])(void) = { (void*)&_estack, Reset_Handler, NMI_Handler, HardFault_Handler, /* ... */ };
```

This is the whole demystification: there is no magic that calls `main`. The **linker script** says *where* `.data`'s init image and the `.bss` range are; the **startup code** uses those symbols to make RAM consistent, then jumps in. Library glue (**newlib-nano**, a size-optimized newlib) and **semihosting** stubs (`_write`, `_sbrk`) get linked here too — semihosting routes `printf` over the debug probe to your host console, convenient for bring-up but slow and blocking.

items.front: List the four jobs the Reset_Handler performs before main().
items.back: 1) Ensure the stack pointer is set (Cortex-M hardware loads MSP from vector[0]). 2) Copy .data from its flash LMA to its RAM VMA. 3) Zero the .bss range. 4) Run __libc_init_array (C++ ctors/init), then call main().
items.hint: SP, copy .data, zero .bss, init+main.

items.prompt: On Cortex-M, how is the initial stack pointer established at reset?
choices: The Reset_Handler must load SP from a constant before anything else
Hardware loads MSP from the first word of the vector table automatically, before the Reset_Handler runs
The linker writes SP into a CPU fuse
SP is left undefined until main sets it
items.explanation: Cortex-M is unusual: at reset the core reads vector[0] as the initial MSP and vector[1] as the reset address, loading both in hardware. That is why vectors[0] is set to &_estack. On many other cores the startup code must set SP manually as its first instruction.

items.prompt: If the startup code skips the `.data` copy step, what symptom appears?
choices: Compilation fails
Zero-initialized globals become nonzero
Initialized globals (e.g. `int baud = 115200;`) read uninitialized/garbage values at run time
The vector table moves to RAM
items.explanation: Initialized globals have their values stored at the flash LMA and addresses in RAM (VMA). Without the copy, the RAM holds whatever was there at power-on, so 'baud' is garbage. This is a classic 'works in debugger that auto-loads, fails standalone' bug.

items.prompt: What breaks if `.bss` is never zeroed at startup?
choices: Nothing; the C runtime always zeroes RAM
All `static`/global zero-initialized variables start with random RAM contents instead of 0
The stack pointer is wrong
Constants in .rodata get corrupted
items.explanation: The C standard requires objects with static storage duration and no explicit initializer to be zero. Those live in .bss, which has no stored bytes; only the startup loop makes them zero. Skip it and 'static int count;' holds garbage, breaking counters, flags, and pointers assumed NULL.

items.front: What are entries [0] and [1] of the Cortex-M vector table?
items.back: [0] = initial Main Stack Pointer value (typically &_estack, top of RAM). [1] = address of the Reset_Handler (initial PC). The core loads these at reset. Subsequent entries are NMI, HardFault, and the rest of the exception/IRQ handlers.
items.hint: Initial SP, then reset PC.

items.front: What does `__libc_init_array()` do, and why call it before main?
items.back: It walks the .init_array/.preinit_array tables and runs each registered initializer: C++ global/static constructors and functions marked __attribute__((constructor)). Calling it before main ensures global objects are constructed before any code uses them.
items.hint: Runs C++ ctors / constructor attributes.

items.front: What is newlib-nano and when do you choose it?
items.back: A size-optimized build of newlib (the common bare-metal libc) with a smaller printf/scanf, reduced reentrancy structures, and a lighter malloc. You select it with --specs=nano.specs to shrink flash/RAM on memory-constrained MCUs, accepting reduced features (e.g. no %f in printf unless you re-enable it).
items.hint: Smaller libc via nano.specs.

items.prompt: What is semihosting and its main drawback?
choices: A way to run two OSes; it doubles RAM use
A mechanism where the target traps to the attached debug host to perform I/O (e.g. printf to the host console); it is slow and blocking, and halts if no debugger is attached
A flash compression scheme; it corrupts .data
A JTAG-only feature that disables SWD
items.explanation: Semihosting uses a special instruction (BKPT 0xAB) to ask the debugger to service syscalls like _write, so printf appears on the host. It is great for bring-up but each call stalls the core waiting on the host, badly distorts timing, and hangs if no debugger services the trap. RTT/defmt are the low-overhead alternatives.

items.front: Explain how the linker script and startup together make main() reachable.
items.back: The linker script defines memory regions, places code/constants in flash, assigns .data a RAM VMA with a flash LMA, sizes .bss, and exports symbols (_sidata,_sdata,_edata,_sbss,_ebss,_estack). The startup/Reset_Handler reads those symbols to copy .data flash->RAM, zero .bss, run init, and branch to main. No hidden runtime does it for you.
items.hint: Linker places + exports symbols; startup uses them.

lessons.title: Flashing, Debug Probes & the Modern Rust Toolchain
lessons.content:
With an `.elf` built, you need it on-chip and a way to inspect a running core. The build emits three artifacts: the **`.elf`** carries sections, symbols, and debug info (what GDB loads); **`objcopy -O binary`** strips it to a raw **`.bin`** (just the bytes, you supply the base address); **`-O ihex`** makes an **`.hex`** (Intel HEX, addresses embedded as ASCII records). Flashers consume `.bin`/`.hex`; debuggers want the `.elf`.

On-chip debug uses one of two transports. **JTAG** is the classic 4-5 pin boundary-scan chain (TCK/TMS/TDI/TDO/TRST), able to daisy-chain many devices. **SWD** (Serial Wire Debug) is the ARM 2-pin replacement (SWCLK + SWDIO) carrying the same Debug Access Port over fewer pins — the default on Cortex-M and what frees up the JTAG pins for I/O. Both are driven by a **probe**: ST-Link (on Nucleo/Discovery), SEGGER J-Link, or open CMSIS-DAP designs like the RP2040 **Picoprobe**.

Two software stacks bridge probe to GDB. **OpenOCD** speaks to the probe and exposes a GDB server; **probe-rs** is the modern Rust-native replacement that also serves GDB and powers `cargo-embed`/`cargo flash`. A GDB session connects, loads, sets **breakpoints/watchpoints**, and examines memory. Crucially, a **CMSIS-SVD** file describes every peripheral register by name, so register-aware debuggers (`svd` in probe-rs, GDB plugins) show `GPIOA->ODR` symbolically instead of as a raw address.

The **modern Rust** path collapses much of this:

```
rustup target add thumbv7em-none-eabihf   # Cortex-M4F
cargo embed --release                      # build + flash + RTT console
```

The `thumbv7em-none-eabihf` triple mirrors `arm-none-eabi`: `thumb` ISA, `v7em` = Cortex-M4/M7, `none` = no OS, `eabihf` = hard-float ABI. `cortex-m-rt` provides the linker script glue and `#[entry]` (the Rust startup). For logging, **defmt** + **RTT** beat semihosting decisively: the device writes compact deferred-format tokens into a RAM ring buffer that the probe drains over the existing debug link — microsecond-scale, non-blocking, and the formatting strings stay on the host, so almost nothing lands in flash.

items.front: Contrast .elf, .bin, and .hex.
items.back: .elf: full object with sections, symbols, debug info — loaded by GDB. .bin: raw contiguous bytes with no address metadata — you must tell the flasher the base address. .hex (Intel HEX): ASCII records that embed addresses, so it is self-locating for flashers. Make .bin/.hex with objcopy -O binary / -O ihex.
items.hint: Symbols vs raw bytes vs addressed-ASCII.

items.prompt: What is the primary practical advantage of SWD over JTAG on a Cortex-M?
choices: SWD is faster in every case and supports more devices in a chain
SWD uses only two pins (SWCLK + SWDIO) to reach the same Debug Access Port, freeing the other JTAG pins for application I/O
SWD does not need a probe
SWD can flash without a debugger
items.explanation: SWD is ARM's 2-wire alternative to JTAG's 4-5 wires, accessing the same Debug Access Port. The win is pin count: it frees TDI/TDO/etc. for GPIO/alternate functions. JTAG keeps the edge of multi-device boundary-scan chains, but for a single Cortex-M, SWD is the usual choice.

items.front: Name three common debug probes and what category each is.
items.back: ST-Link: ST's probe, built onto Nucleo/Discovery boards. SEGGER J-Link: commercial, fast, broad target support (RTT/Ozone). CMSIS-DAP: ARM's open standard, e.g. the RP2040 Picoprobe or DAPLink boards. All present a SWD/JTAG interface that OpenOCD or probe-rs can drive.
